comments_critique.summary: The discussion revolves around various frustrating and unusual volume control UI designs, with users sharing personal experiences and examples from different platforms. Some comments critique the article's originality, while others provide thoughtful insights on design principles and usability issues.

comments.author: Sohcahtoa82
comments.text:
> Should is interesting because of its subjectiveness. It’s a question that only makes sense to be asked in first person. And you have to know about much more than just design to be able to answer it — you have to understand about business, technology, culture, people. Answering the should question is a skill you only get after many, many years answering questions alike.

I wish more front-end designers would consider "should" more often.

"Oh, we can make the scrollbars in our web page auto-hide so PC users get the same experience as Mac users"

But should you?

No. Because one of the reasons I use a PC is because auto-hiding scrollbars on a desktop/laptop is a bug, not a feature, and I disabled that bug while I had a Mac because it's annoying.

"Oh, we can implement smooth scrolling in JavaScript!"

But should you?

No. Because browsers already do it. And your implementation will fail on at least one browser and cause scrolling to just be fucked up. If a user has disabled smooth scrolling, it's probably for a reason. Don't force it back on.

"We can create our own implementation of a drop-down box"

But should you?

No. You're reducing accessibility for literally zero gain. I hate when I'm entering my address, tabbing through the fields, reach the State, and pressing O then R doesn't bring me to "Oregon" or "OR", and instead brings me to Rhode Island. Side note: The order of entering an address is street address, city, state, zip code. If your form order is any different, you're a madman.
comments.analysis: This comment provides a nuanced perspective on design decisions, emphasizing the importance of considering broader contexts beyond just UI, which is a well-founded and insightful critique.

comments.author: semolino
comments.text:
How about the most depraved volume control design of all: the actual reddit web video player (at least the embedded player on old.reddit)?

The slider is hidden by default. Hovering the volume icon makes the slider appear. There is margin between the icon and slider, though, so you have to quickly "zip" your mouse across this gap/chasm before the slider disappears. If you make it over to the slider in time, your hover then preserves its visibility.

I know for sure the devs at Condé ain't dogfoodin' on that interface anymore!
comments.analysis: The comment describes a specific problematic UI behavior with clear examples, likely based on direct experience, making it a credible and relevant contribution.

comments.author: socalgal2
comments.text: I get it's not the same thing but I wish iOS had lower volume settings. As it is, if 100% is max volume then the difference between 0 and unit above 0 on iPhones is about 30% volume. Like, in the middle of the night when everything is quiet, if I was the set it on the lowest setting and make some game sounds I could hear it 2 rooms away with doors open. But, Apple decided you don't need to set it below 30%. Maybe they're trying to force you to buy Airpods
comments.analysis: The comment highlights a real usability issue with iOS volume control granularity, supported by plausible reasoning, though the suggestion about AirPods is speculative.

comments.author: aa-jv
comments.text:
I once worked for a mainstream headphone manufacturer who added a volume control to a product that was so widely despised that a special firmware release had to be done to disable it completely, or else the returns bin would overflow almost overnight ..

So this had me chuckling so hard, having worked professionally in the pro audio world for decades - I can say that some of these 'solutions' would actually be accepted in certain market segments .. I especially love the designs which use a built-in accelerometer.

It seems the good ol' knob is not going anywhere any time soon.
comments.analysis: This comment offers professional insight from industry experience, lending credibility to the critique of unconventional volume controls and supporting the enduring value of traditional knobs.

comments.author: maest
comments.text:
This "article" just rehashes the top submissions from the reddit thread and then adds some surface level musings about UX.

It also blasts you with a full screen subscribe popup, ostensibly in case you want to see more rehashed content.
comments.analysis: This is a valid critique of the article's originality and user experience, though it is somewhat subjective and based on personal reaction to the article's presentation.

comments.author: Dwedit
comments.text: Is there a list of these that are actually in real shipped software and not created as a joke?
comments.analysis: The comment raises a reasonable question about the practicality and real-world usage of the examples, encouraging a more grounded discussion.

comments.author: noisy_boy
comments.text: I think the one with 100 checkboxes with each for a given volume gives direct access to the level of volume you want. Mad, but usable.
comments.analysis: This comment provides a specific example of an unusual but functional UI, contributing to the discussion with a concrete case.

comments.author: nico
comments.text:
Every now and then I get these hilarious volume control videos on TikTok. They show the most horrible ways for doing volume control

One example (you need to play tic tac toe to set the volume) https://www.tiktok.com/@vivancodes/video/7612511893340671240

It seems like that account has quite a few more too
comments.analysis: The comment points to real-world examples of bad volume controls shared on social media, supporting the discussion with external references.

comments.author: Traubenfuchs
comments.text: I feel like it‘s in bad taste to turn a reddit thread into a blog post with zero added value instead of just linking to the thread.
comments.analysis: This comment expresses a subjective opinion on content repurposing, valid as a personal stance but less about the volume control topic itself.

comments.author: terribleperson
comments.text: How about one where the first click sets your volume to max, and then pops up a dialogue to subscribe to a newsletter or sign up for an account? I've never seen such an atrocity, but I could see one plausibly being developed.
comments.analysis: This comment is speculative and humorous rather than factual, serving more as a sarcastic remark than a serious critique.
